title: How to Read Books That Challenge Your Mind: Advice from Robert Greene, Author of The 48 Laws of Power
description: If youвҖҷve fallВӯen out of the habit of readВӯing books, youвҖҷre cerВӯtainВӯly not alone. ConВӯsidВӯer how often posts cirВӯcuВӯlate on social media (i
body:
If youвҖҷve fallВӯen out of the habit of readВӯing books, youвҖҷre cerВӯtainВӯly not alone. ConВӯsidВӯer how often posts cirВӯcuВӯlate on social media (itself a big part of the probВӯlem) about studВӯies showВӯing a rapid increase in the numВӯber of peoВӯple who donвҖҷt even get one book read per year. How best to get back on the litВӯerВӯary wagВӯon? You might try going straight for the hard stuff, as it were, by takВӯing on a novВӯel likeВ Moby-Dick. But that, accordВӯing to the view articВӯuВӯlatВӯed in the video above byВ The 48 Laws of PowВӯer author Robert Greene, would be like stepВӯping back into the gym after years away and tryВӯing to bench press 300 pounds. Rather than startВӯing with MelvilleвҖҷs masВӯterВӯpiece, build your way up to it вҖ” but once you get to it, youвҖҷve got to finВӯish it.
вҖңYou want to train yourВӯself to finВӯish books, and not conВӯstantВӯly be going from one to anothВӯer to anothВӯer,вҖқ Greene says, and that holds even for those you may not enjoy. вҖңWhen I read a book that I hate, that is borВӯing, and I make myself read all the way through, I kind of take angry notes about it: God, this is ridicuВӯlous, this is so stuВӯpid, I hate this, this guy doesВӯnвҖҷt know what heвҖҷs talkВӯing about. You can react to the book, you can have a diaВӯlogue with it, but you want to be able to have the patience to get through a 400- 500, 600-page book.вҖқ To return to the weight-trainВӯing analВӯoВӯgy, a shortВӯer book to start could be вҖңkind of easy, and it could be in a subВӯject that interВӯests you,вҖқ but upon finВӯishВӯing it, you should вҖңchoose someВӯthing thatвҖҷs a litВӯtle bit difВӯfiВӯcult and a litВӯtle bit comВӯpliВӯcatВӯed.вҖқ
The idea is delibВӯerВӯateВӯly to choose books that chalВӯlenge you, a qualВӯiВӯty that doesВӯnвҖҷt come from length alone. If one gives you an impresВӯsion that вҖңitвҖҷs not how you feel politВӯiВӯcalВӯly about the world, itвҖҷs not how you feel spirВӯiВӯtuВӯalВӯly about the world, itвҖҷs someВӯthing thatвҖҷs a litВӯtle bit outВӯside what you would norВӯmalВӯly encounter,вҖқ take it as a sign that you should read it. ApproachВӯing the matВӯter from the othВӯer direcВӯtion, Greene also advisВӯes not to вҖңjust choose things that you think are enterВӯtainВӯing and fun, because thatвҖҷs going to make you lazy, and itвҖҷs going to make you weak, and itвҖҷs going to make you always look for things that are enterВӯtainВӯing, fun, and disВӯtractВӯing.вҖқ An airВӯport-thriller-heavy diet may work for MalВӯcolm GladВӯwell, but itвҖҷs unlikeВӯly to work for you.
As an examВӯple of a chalВӯlengВӯing read, Greene points to any of Robert CaroвҖҷs four bioВӯgraphВӯiВӯcal volВӯumes about U.S. PresВӯiВӯdent LynВӯdon B. JohnВӯson, one of which runs to well over 1,000 pages. He could also have sugВӯgestВӯed CaroвҖҷs earВӯliВӯerВ The PowВӯer BroВӯker: Robert Moses and the Fall of New York, one of those books вҖ” alongВӯside not justВ Moby-Dick, but also the Bible,В Ulysses,В War and Peace,В and even Pride and PrejВӯuВӯdiceВ вҖ”В that even many seriВӯous readВӯers only preВӯtend to have made it through, or indeed startВӯed. WhatВӯevВӯer the mateВӯrВӯiВӯal you use, Greene says,В вҖңI beg you to train your mind like you train your body, to go through someВӯthing thatвҖҷs a litВӯtle more difВӯfiВӯcult, a litВӯtle more chalВӯlengВӯing, thatвҖҷs going to take some time for you. ThatвҖҷs the way to develВӯop the habit of wantВӯiВӯng to read, and to have the patience to read more books.вҖқ No pain, no gain, as the bodyВӯbuilders say; bear that in mind when you get to MelvilleвҖҷs disВӯtilled course on cetolВӯogy.
RelatВӯed conВӯtent:
How to Read Five Books Per Month & Become a SeriВӯous ReadВӯer: Tips from Deep Work Author Cal NewВӯport
20 Books PeoВӯple PreВӯtend to Read (and Now Your ConВӯfesВӯsions?)
How to Read Many More Books in a Year: Watch a Short DocВӯuВӯmenВӯtary FeaВӯturВӯing Some of the WorldвҖҷs Most BeauВӯtiВӯful BookВӯstores
5 Books You Can Read Again .вҖҰ and Again and Again: HereвҖҷs Our Picks, Now Yours
7 Tips for ReadВӯing More Books in a Year
The Nature of Human StuВӯpidВӯiВӯty Explained by The 48 Laws of PowВӯer Author Robert Greene
Based in Seoul,В ColВӯinВ MarshallВ writes and broadВӯcasts on cities, lanВӯguage, and culВӯture. HeвҖҷs the author of the newsletВӯterВ Books on CitiesВ as well as the booksВ н•ңкөӯ мҡ”м•Ҫ кёҲм§ҖВ (No SumВӯmaВӯrizВӯing Korea) andВ KoreВӯan Newtro.В FolВӯlow him on the social netВӯwork forВӯmerВӯly known as TwitВӯter atВ @colinmarshall.

schema.headline: How to Read Books That Challenge Your Mind: Advice from Robert Greene, Author of The 48 Laws of Power

title: The Fascinating Engineering of the Titanic: How the Great Ocean Liner Was Built
description:
in History, Technology |   	March 16th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When many of us first learned of the RMS TitanВӯic, it was preВӯsentВӯed first
body:
in History, Technology |   	March 16th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When many of us first learned of the RMS TitanВӯic, it was preВӯsentВӯed first as one of hisВӯtoВӯryвҖҷs greatВӯest ironies: the вҖңunsinkВӯableвҖқ ocean linВӯer that went down on its maidВӯen voyВӯage. Of course, thereвҖҷs a great deal more to the stoВӯry, as anyВӯone who becomes obsessed with the ill-fatВӯed shipВ (James Cameron being just one notable examВӯple) underВӯstands full well. Even apart from the many human expeВӯriВӯences surВӯroundВӯing it,В some of them told by the wreckВӯвҖҷs surВӯvivors and preВӯserved on film, the mechanВӯiВӯcal aspects of theВ TitanВӯic hold out conВӯsidВӯerВӯable fasВӯciВӯnaВӯtion for anyВӯone with an engiВӯneerвҖҷs cast of mind. Put aside, for the moment, the matВӯter of the sinkВӯing, and conВӯsidВӯer just what went into makВӯing it one of the most gloВӯriВӯous creВӯations of man launched into the ocean to date вҖ” or rather, one of the three most gloВӯriВӯous.
The TitanВӯic was one of a trio of simВӯiВӯlar White Star Line ships comВӯpletВӯed in the earВӯly nineВӯteen-tens. In the video above, Bill HamВӯmack, known on YouTube as EngiВӯneerВӯguy, tells the stoВӯry of not just theВ TitanВӯic, but also theВ Olympic and the HMHS BriВӯtanВӯnic. An engiВӯneerВӯing proВӯfesВӯsor at the UniВӯverВӯsiВӯty of IlliВӯnois, he found in the camВӯpus library issues of the jourВӯnalВ The EngiВӯneer pubВӯlished between 1909 and 1911 that conВӯtain detailed phoВӯtographs of the conВӯstrucВӯtion of bothВ theВ TitanВӯic andВ Olympic, sisВӯter ships that were built side-by-side.
One eleВӯment highВӯlightВӯed that we may not much conВӯsidВӯer today is the sheer scale of the things: each was held togethВӯer by three milВӯlion rivВӯets, could conВӯtain 1.5 milВӯlion galВӯlons of balВӯlast water, weighed 52,000 tons when fulВӯly fitВӯted, required 23 tons of lubriВӯcant to slide from the dock into the water, and burnedВ 650 tons ofВ coal per day on a transatВӯlantic crossВӯing.
Alas, size alone wasВӯnвҖҷt enough to preВӯvent disВӯasВӯter. вҖңLess than a year after the launch of these two giant ships, one sufВӯfered a colВӯliВӯsion that ripped a gapВӯing hole in its side,вҖқ says HamВӯmack. вҖңThat ship was of course, the Olympic.вҖқ Its sudВӯden encounter with a passВӯing warВӯship necesВӯsiВӯtatВӯed patchВӯing with wood before it could return home for a full repair, but thereВӯafter it remained in serВӯvice for nearВӯly a quarВӯter-cenВӯtuВӯry. Its less lucky sibВӯlingВ endВӯed up at the botВӯtom of the ocean after runВӯning into trouВӯble of its own: a mine and a torВӯpeВӯdo spelled the end forВ the BriВӯtanВӯnicВ in 1916. As for theВ TitanВӯic, we all know about its fateВӯful encounter with the iceВӯberg, and maybe weвҖҷve even heard disВӯcusВӯsions of how its designВӯers could have mitВӯiВӯgatВӯed the impact: more or taller bulkВӯheads, a douВӯble hull rather than just a douВӯble botВӯtom, greater lifeboat capacВӯiВӯty. As for whether and how those soluВӯtions would have worked, perВӯhaps HamВӯmack could still shoot a folВӯlow-up explainВӯing it all to us.
RelatВӯed ConВӯtent:
The First Full 3D Scan of the TitanВӯic, Made of More Than 700,000 Images CapВӯturВӯing the WreckвҖҷs Every Detail
See the First 8K Footage of the TitanВӯic, the HighВӯest-QualВӯiВӯty Video of the ShipВӯwreck Yet
The TitanВӯic: Rare Footage of the Ship Before DisВӯasВӯter Strikes (1911вҖ“1912)
TitanВӯic SurВӯvivor InterВӯviews: What It Was Like to Flee the SinkВӯing LuxВӯuВӯry LinВӯer
The SinkВӯing of the BriВӯtanВӯnic: An AniВӯmatВӯed IntroВӯducВӯtion to the TitanВӯicвҖҷs ForВӯgotВӯten SisВӯter Ship
How a 16th-CenВӯtuВӯry ExplorerвҖҷs SailВӯing Ship Worked: An AniВӯmatВӯed Video Takes You on a ComВӯpreВӯhenВӯsive Tour
Based in Seoul,В ColВӯinВ MarshallВ writes and broadВӯcasts on cities, lanВӯguage, and culВӯture. HeвҖҷs the author of the newsletВӯterВ Books on CitiesВ as well as the booksВ н•ңкөӯ мҡ”м•Ҫ кёҲм§ҖВ (No SumВӯmaВӯrizВӯing Korea) andВ KoreВӯan Newtro.В FolВӯlow him on the social netВӯwork forВӯmerВӯly known as TwitВӯter atВ @colinmarshall.

title: White Lace Proliferates Across Urban Spaces in Patterned Murals by NeSpoon
description: NeSpoon continues to embellish the sides residential complexes and facades of historic buildings around the world with her striking murals. Taking
body:
NeSpoon continues to embellish the sides residential complexes and facades of historic buildings around the world with her striking murals. Taking the deceptively simple concept of lace and translating it to an architectonic scale, the artist adds a striking element of whimsy and a nod to craft that peek out around city corners.
As far as we can tell, lace has been around since at least the early 16th century, and it may have originated in Venice. Over the centuries, as the practice evolved and its popularity grew, so did its styles and the techniques used to create it. ThereвҖҷs bobbin lace, which involves fiber attached to small bobbins, which are used to braid or twist the thread into a delicate fabric. And then thereвҖҷs needlelace, which evolved from a number of other kinds of stitched techniques.

NeSpoon uses paint and the power of contrast to create large-scale lace patterns in a celebration of the craft. Often symmetrical, they appear framed by the outlines of corners and roofs, while windows and doors emphasize the muralsвҖҷ scale. From a distance, the patterns appear flawless, as if they could be printed. Up close, itвҖҷs clear the lines are sprayed and brushed by hand, emphasizing the handmade.
Among several art festivals this year, NeSpoon is headed to Valence, France, in May to transform several connected buildings across three blocks. And she is among 100 artists invited to create a work for the reopening of the Pergamonmuseum in Berlin to mark the reopening of the institution in 2027 after an extended renovation. вҖңIt means a lot to me, as the Pergamonmuseum was the first museum I ever visited as a child,вҖқ the artist says.
See more on NeSpoonвҖҷs Instagram and YouTube channel. You may also enjoy the ancient art of kumihomo, a traditional Japanese braiding technique that uses bobbins.

schema.headline: White Lace Proliferates Across Urban Spaces in Patterned Murals by NeSpoon

title: Seurat and the Sea Is Postcard Perfect
description:
Art Review
                Painted during summer trips to the Channel coast, Seurat intended his seascapes to вҖңcleanse oneвҖҷs eye
body:
Art Review
                Painted during summer trips to the Channel coast, Seurat intended his seascapes to вҖңcleanse oneвҖҷs eyes of the days spent in the studio.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Georges Seurat, "Le Bec du Hoc, (Grandcamp)" (1885, reworked around 1888вҖ“89) (all photos Olivia McEwan/Hyperallergic)
    
        
            LONDON вҖ” Whether by accident or design, the Courtauld GalleryвҖҷs Seurat and the Sea immediately follows the National GalleryвҖҷs major survey on the same artistic movement. At least one painting came directly from that show without stopping at home first. While the NationalвҖҷs show, drawn from the KrГ¶ller-MГјller collection, examined weighty issues of socialism and politics, this вҖ” purportedly the UKвҖҷs first exhibition dedicated to the artistвҖҷs seascapes вҖ” is more like a presentation of very nice postcards.This is not to belittle it in the slightest, for the paintings were conceived as such. Of the 45 or so canvases that Seurat produced in his lifetime, we are told that over half are seascapes painted during annual summer trips to the Channel coast between 1885 and 1890, and he intended them to вҖңcleanse oneвҖҷs eyes of the days spent in the studio.вҖқThe narrow subject matter doubly exposes pointillismвҖҷs limitations as a technique. As in the National show, this one includes a diagram explaining how Seurat used opposing color theory to create a shimmering optical illusion of tonal modeling through innumerable short strokes and, later, dots of pigment. While the Impressionists, many of whom are represented in the CourtauldвҖҷs collection, achieved that zingy and highly variable seaside light through expertly selected contrasting pastels, the visual brilliance of SeuratвҖҷs work comes from the masses of closely scattered dots. The color itself is mainly limited to contrasting primary tones, yet the full pictorial space is minutely exploded all over.В Georges Seurat, "The Channel of Gravelines, and Evening" (1890)Georges Seurat, "Study for 'The Channel of Gravelines, an Evening'" (1890)The exhibition really succeeds with its preparatory versions of the larger completed paintings. Compare, for example, вҖңLe Bec du Hoc (Grandcamp)вҖқ from the Tate and its miniature study version from the National Gallery of Australia, Canberra (both 1885). In the study, the singular beak-like rock formation is rendered in larger вҖңpointsвҖқ of relatively consistent distribution and direction, with slightly elongated horizontal dashes defining the sea area. For the final version, the angular dash direction is dialed up a notch, and the diagonal hatching is more distinct, resulting in a chaotic atmosphere. Similarly, the presence of a preparatory study for вҖңThe Channel of Gravelines: An EveningвҖқ (1890) from the Victoria & Albert Museum is fascinatingly alarming because it is the visual and formal antithesis of its finished version at the Museum of Modern Art in New York; in the study, smooth Conte crayon outlines silhouetted anchors against a flatly delineated beach.В That the captions for individual works constantly return to the same refrain вҖ” how pointillism works and its essential theory вҖ” indicates how little artistic, intellectual, or academic depth there is to chomp into here. They read, for instance, вҖңSeuratвҖҷs radical technique consisted of juxtaposing dashes and dots of unmixed colour on the canvasвҖқ and вҖңEach colour appears in the painting in varying intensities, harmonising the scene as a whole.вҖқ These statements could be applied interchangeably to nearly any piece. This is not a bad thing, for the showвҖҷs real takeaway is the meditative and unbending consistency with which Seurat applied his technique, committed and undeviating even when on holiday. Let the artworks wash over you pleasantly like their seaside vistas.В Georges Seurat, "The Semaphones and the Cliff" (1888, reworked around 1889)Georges Seurat, "The Channel of Gravelines, Petit-Fort-Philippe" (1890)Seurat and the Sea continues at the Courtauld Gallery (Somerset House, London, England) through May 17. The exhibition was curated by Karen Serres.
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Reviews
                      
                      
                        Georges Seurat
                      
                      
                        Courtauld Gallery
                      
                      
                        London

schema.headline: Seurat and the Sea Is Postcard Perfect

title: German Hypocrisy and Egyptian Blue
description:
Daily Newsletter
                A Berlin journalвҖҷs false leftism, Egyptian blue paint at Pompeii, looting at the Sudan National
body:
Daily Newsletter
                A Berlin journalвҖҷs false leftism, Egyptian blue paint at Pompeii, looting at the Sudan National Museum, and how can artists get gallery representation?
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            It's no secret that German political hypocrisy runs deep, and the art world is no exception. Berlin-based platform and journal OnCurating published a special edition last fall that bears this pattern out вҖ” virtue-signaling and all. Scholars Angela Harutyunyan and Ana Teixeira Pinto read it so you don't have to. Their opinion, on the other hand, is a sharp critique of antisemitism and liberal ideals weaponized against Palestinians.Meanwhile, Paddy Johnson returns with an Art Problems column addressing the question on so many artists' minds: How do I get gallery representation? That and more below, including a report on the looting of the Sudan National Museum amid the genocide and a story for fans of Egyptian blue.вҖ”Lakshmi Rivera Amin, associate editor
A вҖңFree PalestineвҖқ sticker is stuck on a traffic light on September 29, 2025 in Berlin, Germany (photo Maja Hitij/Getty Images)On Curating CarnageBy draping the tattered banner of feminism and anti-racism over state machinery, a Berlin publication parrots liberal talking points that demonize the Palestinian cause. | Angela Harutyunyan and Ana Teixeira Pinto
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Deepen Your Creative Practice at Vermont Studio CenterSessions include resident presentations, open studios, and the acclaimed Visiting Artist & Writer program. Applications are open for early 2027.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            NewsThe ancient blue shrine found in Pompeii (Regio IX, Insula 10) has a large amount of Egyptian Blue pigment on the walls of the sacred room. (photo courtesy Italy Ministry of Culture)How much did it cost to paint PompeiiвҖҷs dazzling вҖңBlue RoomвҖқ? A new study has the estimates.According to a new report, over 60% of the Sudan National MuseumвҖҷs holdings had been looted in the two years that the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces had control over the capital city of Khartoum.Getting gallery representation is not as hard as it seems. (edit Shari Flores/Hyperallergic)Art Problems: How Do I Get Gallery Representation?Dreaming of showing at your favorite gallery? Paddy Johnson has the masterplan. 
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Haroutiun Galentz: The Form of ColourA new English-language monograph repositions the ArmenianвҖ“Lebanese painter as a cosmopolitan modernist whose work demands to be read beyond national canons.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            FeaturesRomare BeardenвҖҷs вҖңThe DoveвҖқ (1964) on view at the New York Historical (photo Isa Farfan/Hyperallergic)A Visual Journey Through 150 Years of the Legal Aid SocietyA new display at the NY Historical traces the impact of the largest legal organization for low-income individuals in the United States. | Isa FarfanBeverly BuchananвҖҷs Architecture of CareHer practice was one of embodied noticing вҖ” exploring, feeling out, or reaching longingly for a Southern architectural vernacular. | Megan BickelOpportunities This MonthGiovanni Battista Piranesi, вҖңTwo Workmen at TablesвҖқ (early 1770s) (image courtesy the National Gallery of Art, The Ahmanson Foundation)Residencies, fellowships, grants, and open calls from Vermont Studio Center, the Japanese American National Museum, and more in our March 2026 list of opportunities for artists, writers, and art workers.From the ArchivePainting from the Tomb of Nebamun (image via British Museum/Wikimedia)Egyptian Blue: The First Synthetic PigmentThe first human-made blue pigment emerged in ancient Egypt, then disappeared for centuries until it was rediscovered in Pompeii. | Allison Meier

title: Thomas GainsboroughвҖҷs Portraits of Pride and Prejudice
description:
Art Review
                The English artistвҖҷs paintings work hard to make social hierarchy feel beautiful, even natural.
body:
Art Review
                The English artistвҖҷs paintings work hard to make social hierarchy feel beautiful, even natural. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Thomas Gainsborough, "Mr. and Mrs. Andrews" (c. 1750), oil on canvas (В© The National Gallery, London; all photos courtesy the Frick Collection)
    
        
            The first room of Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture opens on a genre already falling out of favor. The вҖңconversation piecesвҖқ gathered here were considered provincial by the London elite by the time Gainsborough painted them. Working from the Suffolk countryside and trained partly in the archaic conventions of English painter Francis Hayman, he gives вҖңThe Gravenor FamilyвҖқ (1754) a similarly formal stiffness, arranging their figures with a peculiar sameness. Yet вҖңMr. and Mrs. AndrewsвҖқ (1750) demonstrates what Gainsborough could already do that his contemporaries could not: make landscape and clothing equally legible as declarations of ownership and belonging, devoting as much attention to the bountiful farmland as Frances AndrewsвҖҷs delicate dress folds and pink satin mules. When Gainsborough moved to Bath in 1759, he refined his style in sustained dialogue with Flemish painter Anthony van Dyck, translating his dramatically lit garments and aristocratic ease into the terms of Georgian self-fashioning.Thomas Gainsborough, "The Gravenor Family" (c. 1754), oil on canvasAttitudes toward British portraiture have soured in recent decades, a problem curator Aimee Ng named plainly at the exhibition's press preview. These paintings have long read as tired images of the wealthy who benefited from colonization and the labor of enslaved people. The exhibition acknowledges this, while insisting that more is enmeshed in GainsboroughвҖҷs world, making a serious case for the complexity of fashion as a site of social negotiation. In 18th-century Britain, taste was a moral technology. The philosopher Anthony Ashley Cooper, Third Earl of Shaftesbury, for instance, argued that beauty and goodness were intertwined, that to cultivate aesthetic discernment was to cultivate virtue. In a period when colonial trade was minting new fortunes faster than existing hierarchies could absorb them, the question of how to distinguish virtuous consumption from vulgar extravagance pressed urgently on critics and patrons alike. Taste promised to regulate luxury, refine appetite, and prove that wealth had earned its elevation. Gainsborough built a career on the visual execution of that promise.Left: Thomas Gainsborough, "Sarah Hodges, Later Lady Innes" (c. 1759), oil on canvas (photo Michael Bodycomb); right: Thomas Gainsborough, "Mary, Countess Howe" (1763вҖ“64), oil on canvas (В© Historic England / Bridgeman Images)A portrait of the young Sarah Hodges from 1759 depicts her with a single rosebud between her fingers and sheer fabric cascading down her sleeves, a debutante on the verge of visibility. Nearby, вҖңMary, Countess HoweвҖқ (1763вҖ“64) strides through a landscape in a storm of pink silk and a perfectly rendered lace apron that folds back on itself with impossible clarity, dressed to the height of fashion from her string of pearls to her heeled shoes. These women demonstrate that they belong in the company of those who, in English writer Samuel Johnson's contemporaneous definition, occupy вҖңa condition above the vulgar,вҖқ where taste supposedly polishes wealth into virtue. Youth and status become questions of taste, calibrated in rosy cheeks and gossamer fabrics, a jeweled brooch or red shoe peeking out from a muslin skirt.This calculus of status as taste extended so far that Gainsborough would often rework certain commissions to suit the changing fads. Studies revealed that вҖңMrs. SheridanвҖқ (1783) was repainted once her earlier shepherdess persona became passГ©, and вҖңMrs. Samuel MoodyвҖқ (1779) revised to add her sons and remove a pearl necklace, recasting respectability in a single sweep of paint. The Frick compellingly frames these additions as part of a story of reinvention and self-fashioning.Left: Thomas Gainsborough, "Mrs. Samuel Moody and Her Sons, Samuel and Thomas" (c. 1779, reworked c. 1784), oil on canvas (В© Dulwich Picture Gallery / Bridgeman Images); right: Thomas Gainsborough, "Mrs. Sheridan" (c. 1783, altered 1785вҖ“87), oil on canvasBut the exhibition is at its sharpest when the moralizing framework of Georgian taste begins to fray. One pairing places вҖңMary, Duchess of MontaguвҖқ (1768) opposite вҖңIgnatius SanchoвҖқ (1768), born into enslavement, later a composer, writer, and celebrated intellectual employed in the Montagu household. Sancho appears in a gentleman's coat and waistcoat, hand tucked into his vest. Yet GainsboroughвҖҷs paint handling falters here; SanchoвҖҷs skin tone slides toward the gray-green ground so that his body seems to hover, without firm footing in space. The gesture grants him dignity while exposing the limits of taste as an engine of social transformation. Fashion can reframe Sancho as вҖңof fashionвҖқ for the length of a sitting, but the structures that profit from his labor remain intact.В What lingers is the sense of how hard these images work to make hierarchy feel beautiful, even natural. The porcelain faces, naval uniforms, foliage, and dissolving grounds are symptoms of a world where the subject yields to the performance of good taste. The exhibition offers real pleasures in its meticulous attention to detail: silk that catches light, gold that seems to rustle, the quiet nods to shifting fashions of the social season. But its most urgent contribution lies in making visible the labor of taste itself, and the social fiction it has always sustained: the promise that style can launder wealth into moral distinction. Seen from our present, that promise looks increasingly fragile, and the paintings begin to flicker between high fantasy and indictment.Left: Thomas Gainsborough, "Mary, Duchess of Montagu" (c. 1768), oil on canvas (photo courtesy The Buccleuch Living Heritage Trust); right: Thomas Gainsborough, "Ignatius Sancho" (1768), oil on canvasInstallation view of Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture continues at the Frick Collection (1 East 70th Street) through May 25, 2026. The exhibition was curated by Aimee Ng. 
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Reviews
                      
                      
                        Thomas Gainsborough
                      
                      
                        Frick Collection
                      
                      
                        New York

schema.headline: Thomas GainsboroughвҖҷs Portraits of Pride and Prejudice

title: Film by Artist Alexandre Signh and Art Historian Natalie Musteata Wins Oscar
description: Artists and filmmakers Alexandre Singh and Natalie Musteata won the Oscar for best live action short film on Sunday for their dystopian short Two Pe
body:
Artists and filmmakers Alexandre Singh and Natalie Musteata won the Oscar for best live action short film on Sunday for their dystopian short Two People Exchanging Saliva. In a rare twist, however, they didnвҖҷt take the prize alone. The award ended in a tie with the musical comedy short The Singers.

	Two People Exchanging Saliva runs 36 minutes and is in French, while The Singers, an 18-minute musical comedy directed by Sam Davis, follows a group of strangers who connect through music during a moment of widespread social isolation.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	Comedian and actor Kumail Nanjiani, who presented the award, seemed almost as surprised as the nominees. вҖңItвҖҷs a tieвҖ”IвҖҷm not joking, itвҖҷs actually a tie,вҖқ he said from the stage. вҖңEveryone calm down, weвҖҷre going to get through this, focus up.вҖқ

	Singh and MusteataвҖҷs film premiered at the Telluride Film Festival and has since circulated widely on the international festival circuit. Set in present-day Paris, it imagines a society where kissing, and nearly any form of physical intimacy, is illegal and punishable by death. In this strange economy, customers pay for everyday goods with ritualized slaps delivered to store employees. The story follows Malaise, a department-store saleswoman, and Angine, an elegant regular customer, who struggle to suppress a growing attraction in a world where even minor transgressions can be fatal.

	During his acceptance speech, Singh, who is known for his elaborate performances and narrative artworks, used the opportunity to take a light jab at recent comments by TimothГ©e Chalamet. вҖңWe believe that art can change peopleвҖҷs soulsвҖҰ we can change society through art and creativity, through theatre and ballet,вҖқ he told the audience. Afterward, Musteata described the win simply: вҖңItвҖҷs a dream.вҖқ

	Singh has previously exhibited with galleries including SprГјth Magers and the now-closed Metro Pictures. Musteata, a writer and curator with a PhD in art history, has collaborated with him on numerous projects across exhibitions, films, and publications, including the exhibition вҖңA Gothic TaleвҖқ and the short film The Appointment.

schema.headline: Film by Artist Alexandre Signh and Art Historian Natalie Musteata Wins Oscar
